question: Two Sum
answer:
Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers such that they add up to the target.

Optimal Approach: Use a HashMap to store each number and its index while iterating. For each element, check if target - nums[i] already exists in the map.

  • Time Complexity: O(n)
  • Space Complexity: O(n)
tip: Always think of HashMap whenever you need fast lookups in array problems.
deep:
Problem intuition

The goal is to find two numbers whose sum equals the target. The naive solution compares every pair, but that's inefficient. The key observation is that for every number x, we only need to know whether target - x has already been seen.

Approaches

  • Brute Force: Compare every pair using two loops. Time Complexity: O(n²).
  • Optimal: Store visited numbers in a HashMap. Before inserting the current number, check if its complement already exists.
nums = [2,7,11,15]
target = 9

Map = {}
2 → complement = 7 → not found
Store 2

7 → complement = 2 → found
Answer = [0,1]

Why HashMap works

HashMap provides average O(1) lookup time. Instead of searching the remaining array for every element, we instantly know whether the required complement already exists.

Common mistakes

  • Adding the current element to the map before checking its complement.
  • Returning the values instead of their indices.
  • Using nested loops after already creating a HashMap.

Interview tip

Whenever you hear phrases like find a pair, target sum, or fast lookup, think of using a HashMap.

question: Valid Parentheses
answer:
Given a string containing only (), [], and {}, determine if the input string is valid.

Optimal Approach: Use a Stack. Push opening brackets onto the stack. Whenever a closing bracket appears, check if it matches the top of the stack.

  • Time Complexity: O(n)
  • Space Complexity: O(n)
tip: Whenever the problem asks about matching pairs or nested structures, think of a Stack.
deep:
Problem intuition

This problem asks whether every opening bracket has a matching closing bracket in the correct order. Since brackets must close in reverse order of opening, a Stack is the perfect data structure.

Approaches

  • Brute Force: Repeatedly remove matching pairs like (), [], and {} until nothing changes. This is inefficient.
  • Optimal: Push every opening bracket onto a stack. Whenever a closing bracket appears, verify it matches the top of the stack.
Input: "({[]})"

Stack = []
( → push
{ → push
[ → push
] → pop [
} → pop {
) → pop (

Stack empty → Valid

Why Stack works

A stack follows the Last In, First Out (LIFO) principle. The most recently opened bracket must always be closed first, making Stack the ideal choice.

Common mistakes

  • Not checking if the stack is empty before popping.
  • Forgetting to verify the stack is empty after processing the entire string.
  • Trying to count brackets instead of checking their order.

Interview tip

Whenever the problem involves nested structures, balanced symbols, or matching pairs, your first thought should be a Stack.

question: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
answer:
Given an array prices where prices[i] is the stock price on day i, find the maximum profit by choosing one day to buy and one later day to sell.

Optimal Approach: Traverse the array once while maintaining the minimum price seen so far. At each step, calculate the profit if you sold today and update the maximum profit.

  • Time Complexity: O(n)
  • Space Complexity: O(1)
tip: Track the minimum price so far, not every possible buy day. One pass is enough.
deep:
Problem intuition

You must buy before selling, and you can complete only one transaction. Instead of checking every buy-sell pair, keep track of the cheapest stock price seen so far.

Brute Force

Try every pair of buy and sell days using two loops.

  • Time Complexity: O(n²)
  • Space Complexity: O(1)

Optimal Approach

Maintain two variables:

  • minPrice → Lowest stock price seen so far.
  • maxProfit → Best profit found so far.
prices = [7,1,5,3,6,4]

Day 1: min=7 profit=0
Day 2: min=1 profit=0
Day 3: profit=5-1=4
Day 4: profit=3-1=2
Day 5: profit=6-1=5
Day 6: profit=4-1=3

Maximum Profit = 5

Why it works

At every day, we know the cheapest buying price available before today. Selling today gives the best possible profit for that day. Taking the maximum over all days gives the answer.

Common mistakes

  • Trying every buy-sell pair (O(n²)).
  • Selling before buying.
  • Returning negative profit when prices always decrease.

Interview Tip

This is a classic Greedy problem. Whenever you need the best difference while scanning an array once, think of maintaining a running minimum or maximum.

question: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
answer:
Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

Optimal Approach: Use the Sliding Window technique along with a HashMap to store the last index of every character. Whenever a duplicate character is found inside the current window, move the left pointer just after its previous occurrence.

  • Time Complexity: O(n)
  • Space Complexity: O(min(n, charset))
tip: Whenever you hear longest/shortest substring with a condition, think Sliding Window.
deep:
Problem intuition

We need the longest contiguous substring where every character is unique. Instead of checking every possible substring, maintain a moving window that always contains unique characters.

Approaches

  • Brute Force: Generate every substring and check if it contains duplicates. Time Complexity: O(n³).
  • Better: Check uniqueness using a HashSet for every starting index. Time Complexity: O(n²).
  • Optimal: Use a Sliding Window with a HashMap storing each character's latest index.
s = "abcabcbb"

left = 0
right = 0 → a → window = "a"
right = 1 → b → window = "ab"
right = 2 → c → window = "abc"
right = 3 → a (duplicate)
Move left to index after previous 'a'
window = "bca"
Continue...

Maximum Length = 3

Why Sliding Window works

The window always maintains unique characters. Whenever a duplicate is found, we shrink the window only as much as needed, ensuring every character is processed at most twice.

Common mistakes

  • Moving the left pointer backwards instead of using Math.max().
  • Using a HashSet but removing characters one by one unnecessarily.
  • Checking all substrings instead of maintaining a valid window.

Interview Tip

Whenever the problem mentions longest substring, unique characters, or contiguous sequence, your first thought should be Sliding Window.

question: Contains Duplicate
answer:
Given an integer array nums, return true if any value appears at least twice in the array, and return false if every element is distinct.

Optimal Approach: Use a HashSet. As you iterate through the array, check if the current element already exists in the set. If it does, a duplicate has been found.

  • Time Complexity: O(n)
  • Space Complexity: O(n)
tip: Whenever you need to detect duplicates quickly, think HashSet.
deep:
Problem intuition

The task is simply to determine whether any number appears more than once. Instead of comparing every pair, remember the numbers you've already seen.

Approaches

  • Brute Force: Compare every pair using nested loops. Time Complexity: O(n²).
  • Sorting: Sort the array and check adjacent elements. Time Complexity: O(n log n).
  • Optimal: Store visited elements in a HashSet. If insertion fails, a duplicate exists.
nums = [1,2,3,1]

Set = {}
1 → add
2 → add
3 → add
1 → already exists

Answer = true

Why HashSet works

A HashSet provides average O(1) insertion and lookup. This allows us to detect duplicates in a single pass through the array.

Common mistakes

  • Using nested loops instead of a HashSet.
  • Sorting unnecessarily when only duplicate detection is required.
  • Using a List instead of a Set, resulting in slower lookups.

Interview Tip

Whenever you hear phrases like contains duplicate, unique elements, or already seen, think of using a HashSet.

question: Valid Anagram
answer:
Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s, otherwise return false.

Optimal Approach: Since the strings contain only lowercase English letters, use an integer frequency array of size 26. Increment the count for each character in s and decrement for each character in t. If all counts become zero, the strings are anagrams.

  • Time Complexity: O(n)
  • Space Complexity: O(1)
tip: Whenever the input contains only lowercase English letters, prefer an int[26] over a HashMap.
deep:
Problem intuition

Two strings are anagrams if they contain exactly the same characters with the same frequencies. Instead of sorting both strings, simply count how many times each letter appears.

Approaches

  • Sorting: Sort both strings and compare. Time Complexity: O(n log n).
  • Optimal: Maintain a frequency array of size 26. Increase counts for one string and decrease for the other.
s = "anagram"
t = "nagaram"

Count['a']++ / Count['a']--
Count['n']++ / Count['n']--
...

All counts become 0
Answer = true

Why Frequency Array works

Each increment is canceled by a matching decrement. If every character appears the same number of times, all values become zero.

Common mistakes

  • Using HashMap when only lowercase letters are present.
  • Forgetting to compare string lengths first.
  • Sorting when an O(n) solution exists.

Interview Tip

When the character set is fixed (26 lowercase letters), an array is almost always faster than a HashMap.

question: Merge Sorted Array
answer:
You are given two sorted integer arrays nums1 and nums2. Merge nums2 into nums1 as one sorted array.

Optimal Approach: Use three pointers starting from the end of both arrays. Compare the largest remaining elements and place the larger one at the end of nums1.

  • Time Complexity: O(m+n)
  • Space Complexity: O(1)
tip: Whenever you merge into an existing array, fill it from the end to avoid overwriting values.
deep:
Problem intuition

Since both arrays are already sorted, the largest element must always be at the end of one of the arrays. Filling from the end prevents overwriting valid values inside nums1.

Approaches

  • Extra Array: Merge into a new array and copy back.
  • Optimal: Use three pointers from the end.
nums1 = [1,2,3,0,0,0]
nums2 = [2,5,6]

Compare 3 and 6 → place 6
Compare 3 and 5 → place 5
Compare 3 and 2 → place 3
...

Result = [1,2,2,3,5,6]

Why it works

The unused space is already at the end of nums1, making backward traversal ideal.

Common mistakes

  • Merging from the front and overwriting values.
  • Forgetting to copy remaining elements from nums2.
  • Using unnecessary extra arrays.

Interview Tip

Whenever merging into an existing array with extra space, think backward two pointers.

question: Squares of a Sorted Array
answer:
Given a sorted integer array nums, return an array of the squares of each number sorted in non-decreasing order.

Optimal Approach: Use two pointers at the beginning and end of the array. Compare the absolute values and place the larger square at the end of the result array.

  • Time Complexity: O(n)
  • Space Complexity: O(n)
tip: Whenever negative and positive numbers are sorted, compare their absolute values using two pointers.
deep:
Problem intuition

Although the input array is sorted, squaring negative numbers changes their order. The largest square always comes from either the leftmost negative number or the rightmost positive number.

Approaches

  • Brute Force: Square every element and sort again. Time Complexity: O(n log n).
  • Optimal: Compare absolute values using two pointers.
nums = [-4,-1,0,3,10]

Compare |-4| and |10| → 100
Compare |-4| and |3| → 16
Compare |-1| and |3| → 9
...

Result = [0,1,9,16,100]

Why Two Pointers work

The maximum square always comes from one of the two ends of the sorted array. Fill the result array from right to left.

Common mistakes

  • Squaring first and assuming the array remains sorted.
  • Sorting after squaring instead of using two pointers.
  • Comparing actual values instead of absolute values.

Interview Tip

Whenever the input is sorted but a transformation changes the order, think about using two pointers instead of sorting again.
